annotations.label: Rational function (informal)

anchor.snippet: a rational function is any function that can be defined by a rational fraction

annotations.note: `RatFunc K` is Mathlib's `K(X)`, the field of rational fractions over a commutative ring/field K.

annotations.label: Field of rational functions

anchor.snippet: The set of rational functions over a field K is a field

annotations.note: The `Field K⟮X⟯` instance over a domain is provided as `RatFunc.instField`.

annotations.label: Rational function

anchor.snippet: is called a rational function if it can be written in the form

annotations.note: `RatFunc.mk p q` constructs the rational function p/q from polynomials p,q.

annotations.label: Domain of a rational function

anchor.snippet: The domain of

annotations.note: `RatFunc.eval` evaluates a rational function but its lemmas only require `denom ≠ 0`; the precise notion of domain-of-definition is not packaged as a definition.

annotations.label: Equivalence of rational fractions

anchor.snippet: one can define a rational fraction as an equivalence class of fractions of polynomials

annotations.note: `RatFunc K` is literally a wrapper over `FractionRing K[X]`, the equivalence-class construction.

annotations.label: Proper rational function

anchor.snippet: A proper rational function is a rational function in which the degree of

annotations.note: No predicate for `deg num < deg denom` (proper rational function) is defined in Mathlib's RatFunc API.

annotations.label: Complex rational function

anchor.snippet: is the ratio of two polynomials with complex coefficients

annotations.note: Specializing `RatFunc K` to `K = ℂ` gives complex rational functions; there is no separately named declaration.

annotations.label: Extension to the Riemann sphere

anchor.snippet: Every rational function can be naturally extended to a function whose domain and range are the whole Riemann sphere

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Riemann sphere or canonical extension of rational functions to `OnePoint ℂ`.

annotations.label: Möbius transformation

anchor.snippet: A complex rational function with degree one is a Möbius transformation

annotations.note: The `GL(2,K)` action on `OnePoint K` is given by Möbius transformations, but no standalone `Mobius` declaration is defined.

annotations.label: Rational functions are meromorphic

anchor.snippet: Rational functions are representative examples of meromorphic functions

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `MeromorphicOn` and meromorphic order but does not link `RatFunc ℂ` to meromorphicity by a dedicated lemma.

annotations.label: Degree of a rational function

anchor.snippet: the degree of a rational function is the maximum of the degrees of its constituent polynomials

annotations.note: The quantity `max f.num.natDegree f.denom.natDegree` appears as the RHS of `finrank_eq_max_natDegree` but is not exposed as a named `degree` function on `RatFunc`.

annotations.label: Degree of the graph of a rational function

anchor.snippet: The degree of the graph of a rational function is not the degree as defined above

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of the degree of the graph (closure-in-product) of a rational function.

annotations.label: Asymptotic degree

anchor.snippet: the degree of a rational function is the difference between the degrees of the numerator and the denominator

annotations.note: `RatFunc.intDegree x = natDegree x.num - natDegree x.denom : ℤ` is exactly the asymptotic/difference degree.

annotations.label: Biquadratic function

anchor.snippet: a rational function of degree two (that is, the ratio of two polynomials of degree at most two) is often called a biquadratic function

annotations.note: No biquadratic-function definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rational function undefined at a point

anchor.snippet: The rational function

annotations.note: Specific worked examples of rational functions undefined at a point are not formalized as named declarations.

annotations.label: Real but not complex-defined

anchor.snippet: is defined for all real numbers , but not for all complex numbers

annotations.note: This particular example (e.g. `1/(x²+1)`) is not a named Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Constant function as rational

anchor.snippet: A constant function such as

annotations.note: The algebra map `K → K⟮X⟯` equals `C`, embedding constants as rational functions.

annotations.label: Polynomials as rational functions

anchor.snippet: Every polynomial function

annotations.note: `RatFunc.coePolynomial` (and `algebraMap K[X] K⟮X⟯`) embeds polynomials into the rational function field.

annotations.label: Laurent polynomials as rational functions

anchor.snippet: Every Laurent polynomial can be written as a rational function

annotations.note: No canonical algebra map / coercion `LaurentPolynomial R → RatFunc R` is provided in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Removable singularity

anchor.snippet: is equal to 1 for all x except 0, where there is a removable singularity

annotations.note: The worked example `x/x` with a removable singularity at 0 is not a named declaration.

annotations.label: Closure under sum, product, quotient

anchor.snippet: The sum, product, or quotient (excepting division by the zero polynomial) of two rational functions is itself a rational function

annotations.note: The `Field K⟮X⟯` instance gives closure of `RatFunc K` under `+`, `*`, and `/` (with nonzero denominator).

annotations.label: Taylor coefficients satisfy linear recurrence

anchor.snippet: The coefficients of a Taylor series of any rational function satisfy a linear recurrence relation

annotations.note: Mathlib has `LinearRecurrence` and `RatFunc` but does not connect Taylor/power-series coefficients of a rational function to a linear recurrence.

annotations.label: Linear recurrences determine rational functions

anchor.snippet: any sequence that satisfies a linear recurrence determines a rational function when used as the coefficients of a Taylor series

annotations.note: The converse direction—linearly recurrent sequence yields a rational generating function—is not formalized.

annotations.label: Rational expression over a field

anchor.snippet: a rational expression (also known as a rational fraction or, in algebraic geometry , a rational function ) is any element of the field of fractions of the polynomial ring

annotations.note: `RatFunc K` is defined to be `FractionRing K[X]` (wrapped), the field of fractions of the polynomial ring.

annotations.label: Unique lowest-terms representation

anchor.snippet: there is a unique representation for any rational expression

annotations.note: `RatFunc.numDenom` (together with `RatFunc.isCoprime_num_denom`) produces the canonical coprime/monic-denominator representative.

annotations.label: Field of rational expressions

anchor.snippet: The field of rational expressions is denoted

annotations.note: `K⟮X⟯ = RatFunc K` with `RatFunc.instField` is Mathlib's field of rational expressions.

annotations.label: Rational expressions in several indeterminates

anchor.section: Notion of a rational function on an algebraic variety
anchor.snippet: rational expressions can also be generalized to

annotations.note: Mathlib uses `FractionRing (MvPolynomial σ F)` (the `MvRatFunc` namespace) as the multivariate rational function field but does not provide a dedicated `MvRatFunc` definition.

annotations.label: Function field of an algebraic variety

anchor.section: Notion of a rational function on an algebraic variety
anchor.snippet: the function field of an algebraic variety

annotations.note: `Scheme.functionField X` is the stalk at the generic point of an irreducible scheme, a field when `X` is integral.
